title: Full-Length Mirror with Sliding Storage Cabinet
summary: A framed full-length mirror that rolls sideways on drawer slides to reveal a hidden wall cabinet — built to whatever mirror you already own.
description:
This is two builds wearing one silhouette: a wall-mounted shallow shelf unit, and a framed full-length mirror that hangs in front of it on a pair of full-extension drawer slides — turned sideways. Slide the mirror left, and the cabinet behind appears: hair tools, jewelry, sunscreen, all the shallow clutter a bathroom or closet wall can swallow.

Everything is sized off YOUR mirror, which is why the cut list here is formulas rather than numbers: frame rails at mirror width, frame stiles at mirror height plus 1-1/2", shelf sides the same. Buy the mirror first (a basic full-length wall mirror), then cut.

The drawer-slide trick in the last step is the heart of the build, and the source explains it precisely: a side-mount slide's two halves have their screw holes at the same offset. So draw a line 1-1/2" down from the top of the SHELF unit and the same line 1-1/2" down from the top of the MIRROR frame, center the screw holes of each slide half on its line, and the mirror hangs at exactly shelf height and travels level. Repeat at the bottom. Two lines, no fitting, no shimming. Buy slides shorter than the cabinet's width (a ~15" cabinet takes 14" slides).

Two sequencing notes from the source builder worth stealing: glue the mirror into its frame LAST, after the slides are mounted — the frame is far lighter and easier to handle empty — and set the bottom wall cleat at a height that leaves room for pipe hangers or hooks under the shelves if you want tool storage. Mount the whole thing to studs; a mirror on a moving carriage is not a drywall-anchor project.

tools.note: 1-1/4" brads and glue build both the frame and the shelf unit.
The unit mounts to the wall and the mirror must travel level — the level earns its spot twice.
Clean option for joining the shelf boards to the sides.

materials.name: 1x4, 8 ft
materials.note: Shelf unit sides and shelves. A wider or taller mirror may need a third.

materials.name: 1x2, 8 ft
materials.note: Mirror frame, wall cleats, and slide-mount rails.

materials.name: Full-length wall mirror
materials.note: Buy FIRST — every cut is sized from it.

materials.name: Full-extension side-mount drawer slides, pair
materials.note: Length just under the cabinet width (~15" cabinet → 14" slides). The standard silver ball-bearing kind.

materials.name: Wood screws, 3"
materials.note: Into wall studs. Studs, not anchors — this carries glass on a moving carriage.

materials.name: Construction adhesive or mirror glue
materials.note: Mirror-rated adhesive — ordinary construction adhesive can attack mirror backing.

steps.title: Build the frame's L-rails
steps.body: Every cut in this build is a formula off your mirror. Start with the frame: nail two 1x2s into an 'L' profile with 1-1/4" brads and glue — two L-assemblies, each cut to the mirror's WIDTH. These are the frame's top and bottom; the L-channel is what the mirror will eventually sit in.

steps.title: Complete the mirror frame — empty
steps.body:
Cut two 1x2 stiles at the mirror's HEIGHT plus 1-1/2" and attach them to the L-assemblies, outside edges flush, checking square by matching diagonals. Add two more 1x2 crosspieces flat across the back for support, flush to the back face.

Do NOT glue the mirror in yet. An empty frame weighs nothing and takes drawer slides gracefully; a glass-filled one does neither.

steps.title: Build the shelf unit
steps.body:
Cut two 1x4 sides at mirror height plus 1-1/2" (matching the frame) and five 1x4 shelves at mirror width. Assemble with brads and glue, or pocket holes if you'd rather hide fasteners — shelves spaced for what will live here (hair tools and bottles want different gaps).

The cabinet is shallow by design: 1x4 deep. It hides completely behind the mirror, which is the entire party trick.

steps.title: Add cleats and slide rails
steps.body:
Attach 1x2 rails across the shelf unit's back at top and bottom — these are the wall-mount cleats. Add 1x2s at the FRONT edges where the drawer slides will screw; the slides need flat rail to land on.

Set the bottom cleat's height with intent: the source mounted hers to leave room for pipe hangers holding curling irons under the bottom shelf. Plan your hooks now, while the back is open.

steps.title: The two-line slide trick
steps.body:
Side-mount slides put both halves' screw holes at the same offset — exploit it. Draw a line 1-1/2" down from the top across the shelf unit's slide rail, and a line 1-1/2" down from the top of the mirror frame. Repeat at the bottoms. Separate each slide, center its screw holes on the lines, and drive the screws dead on the line.

Mount the cabinet half of each slide to the shelf unit and the drawer half to the mirror frame, hang the frame on, and test the travel. It should glide level with no drama — if it binds, one screw is off its line; the lines are the jig.

steps.title: Mount to studs, then glue in the mirror
steps.body:
Find the studs, hold the unit level, and drive the 3" screws through the top and bottom cleats into framing. This assembly carries glass that moves — anchors are not invited.

Finish/paint everything, THEN bed the mirror into its frame with mirror-rated adhesive (ordinary construction adhesive can eat mirror backing and bloom black spots). Let it cure per the tube before the first slide. Then enjoy the best kind of storage: the kind guests can't find.

images.alt: Framed full-length mirror slid open to reveal a hidden shallow storage cabinet
